title: Escalation of Care — Decision
oneLiner: A structured process to identify and respond to clinical deterioration, activating senior or critical care resources to prevent cardiac arrest or avoidable harm.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**
A 72-year-old woman, day 2 post-hemicolectomy for colon cancer, is reviewed by the night FY1. She is mildly confused, her respiratory rate is 28, oxygen saturations 90% on 4L nasal cannula, heart rate 110, blood pressure 90/60, temperature 38.5°C, and urine output <20 mL/hour for the last 2 hours. The nursing staff are worried. The FY1 feels out of depth and considers whether to call the registrar.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Confusion** → cerebral hypoperfusion / hypoxia / sepsis-associated encephalopathy – a red flag for organ dysfunction
- **Respiratory rate 28** → earliest and most sensitive sign of deterioration (tachypnoea compensates for metabolic acidosis)
- **SpO₂ 90% on 4L** → gas exchange failure; likely intrapulmonary shunting or worsening pneumonia/ARDS
- **HR 110, BP 90/60** → compensated shock; sympathetic drive is maximal but losing ground
- **Temperature 38.5°C** → infective trigger (though hypothermia also = bad)
- **Oliguria (<20 mL/hr)** → renal hypoperfusion (pre-renal AKI); near-failing perfusion
- **Nursing staff worried** → never ignore clinical instinct from the bedside team – often the earliest pattern cue

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Silent hypoxia:** young patient with COVID-19, saturations 85% but completely alert, tachypnoeic but not distressed. Escalation delayed because patient “looks well” – but RR >24 and SpO₂ <90% is a hard trigger.
- **Isolated hypotension post-op:** BP 75/40, alert, no tachycardia. Could be vasoplegia from spinal anaesthetic or bleeding into drains. Both require escalation – if you assume “it’s just the spinal” you miss haemorrhage.
- **Agitation/psychosis without vital sign change:** elderly patient on opioids becomes restless. Maybe hypoxia or hypercapnia – check ABG before sedating. Early escalation to acute physicians can prevent aspiration.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Escalation of Care is the explicit decision to hand over or share decision-making about a patient whose clinical trajectory is worsening beyond the ability (or scope) of the current team. It is a multi-step judgment that includes recognising deterioration, communicating concern, and activating a higher level of care – typically a senior ward clinician, an outreach team, or intensive care.

**Why it matters:**  
- **Frequency:** ~1 in 20 acute admissions will deteriorate unexpectedly; failure to escalate is the third commonest cause of in-hospital cardiac arrest.
- **Stakes:** Missed escalation leads to preventable ICU admission delay, prolonged organ failure, or death. Conversely, inappropriate “fake escalations” (e.g., calling ICU for a mild UTI) waste resources and erode trust.
- **Common pitfalls:** Overconfidence (“I can fix it myself”), hierarchical fear (“the registrar will be annoyed I called”), and pattern-blindness (“they looked fine an hour ago”). These are **decision failures**, not knowledge gaps – so you need a structured mental model.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
The decision to escalate is driven by **pathophysiological decompensation**, usually from an inflammatory or hypoperfusion state that overwhelms compensatory mechanisms.

**The story:**
- A trigger (infection, bleeding, hypovolaemia, MI) activates the sympathetic nervous system and innate immune response. Catecholamines increase heart rate, contractility, and vasoconstriction. This is **compensated shock** – the patient may be tachycardic, hypertensive, and tachypnoeic. At this stage, early intervention (fluids, antibiotics) can reverse.
- If the insult persists, oxygen delivery falls below demand. Anaerobic metabolism generates lactate, causing metabolic acidosis. The respiratory centre drives tachypnoea (Kussmaul breathing) to blow off CO₂ – this is why **tachypnoea is the earliest sign**.
- As acidosis worsens, myocardial depression sets in. Vasodilation (from cytokines or sepsis) overcomes compensatory vasoconstriction. Blood pressure drops – **hypotension is a late sign** (“the patient has exhausted their reserves”).
- Organs suffer: brain (confusion), kidneys (oliguria), liver (transaminitis, coagulopathy), lungs (ARDS). **The presence of any single organ dysfunction = the patient is no longer compensating**.
- Eventually, ATP depletion causes cell death and irreversible shock. This is why **time matters** – your window to escalate is narrow.

**Mechanism → clinical signs:**
- Reduced cerebral perfusion → confusion, agitation, reduced GCS
- Increased lactate → metabolic acidosis → compensatory tachypnoea
- Renal hypoperfusion → prerenal AKI → oliguria, rising creatinine
- Inadequate oxygen delivery → cyanosis, mottled skin (late)
- Anaerobic metabolism → lactic acidosis → high anion gap on ABG

**Connect to pattern cues:**
In the vignette, the patient has three organ dysfunctions (CNS, renal, respiratory). That is a **red-flag pattern** – she has already decompensated. The FY1 cannot manage this alone; escalation is mandatory.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Infection** | Sepsis (pneumonia, UTI, intra-abdominal, line-related) | Fever/hypothermia + raised WCC/procalcitonin + focal signs |
| **Hypovolaemia** | Haemorrhage (post-op, GI bleed, trauma), dehydration (vomiting, diarrhoea, burns) | Tachycardia, low CVP, drop in Hb, obvious loss |
| **Cardiovascular** | Myocardial infarction, arrhythmia (AF, VT), tamponade, pulmonary embolism | Chest pain, ECG changes, raised troponin, D-dimer |
| **Respiratory** | Pneumothorax, pulmonary oedema, COPD exacerbation, massive PE | Unilateral breath sounds, frothy sputum, known COPD |
| **Metabolic/endocrine** | Diabetic ketoacidosis, Addisonian crisis, hypoglycaemia, thyroid storm | History of diabetes/steroid use, hyperventilation, electrolyte disturbances |
| **Neurological** | Stroke, seizure (status epilepticus), raised ICP, meningitis | Focal neurology, seizure activity, reduced GCS, fever |
| **Toxicological** | Drug overdose (opioids, benzos, digoxin), anaphylaxis, transfusion reaction | Known substance use, laryngeal oedema, skin rash, hypotension |
| **Procedural** | Failed intubation, massive haemorrhage during surgery, post-procedural bleeding | Recent procedure, low BP, high lactate |

**Most common cause** in a general ward: **Sepsis** – triggers a systemic inflammatory response that can cause multi-organ failure within hours.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
**NEWS2 (National Early Warning Score 2)** – the most widely used escalation tool.  
- Score 0–2: normal → routine monitoring  
- Score 3–4: trigger for urgent team review  
- Score 5–6: trigger for immediate review (frequency <1 hour)  
- Score 7+: trigger for critical care outreach / registrar ASAP  
- **Any single parameter scoring 3** (e.g., RR 25–29 = 3, SpO₂ ≤91% on room air = 3) should trigger escalation regardless of total score.

**For each key test:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|---------------|------------------|
| **ABG** | pH, PaCO₂, PaO₂, HCO₃, lactate, base excess | Assess oxygenation, ventilation, and acid-base status | **Lactate >2 mmol/L** = tissue hypoperfusion (→ escalate if >4). PaO₂<60 despite FiO₂>0.5 = refractory hypoxaemia. |
| **Blood cultures** | Bacteraemia | Identify pathogen before antibiotics | **Two sets from different sites** – positive = sepsis source; but do not delay ABx |
| **FBC** | WCC (↑/↓), Hb (↓ in bleeding), platelets | Infection response, anaemia, DIC | **WCC <4 or >20** = high risk sepsis. **Platelets <100** = possible DIC |
| **U&Es** | Creatinine, urea, potassium | Renal function, hydration | **Creatinine ↑ by >26 µmol/L in 48h = AKI Stage 1** → needs fluid and stop nephrotoxins |
| **Lactate** | Anaerobic metabolism | Single best predictor of deterioration | **Lactate >2** is abnormal; >4 = severe sepsis/septic shock → escalate |
| **CXR** | Pneumonia, pneumothorax, oedema | Respiratory focus of infection or complication | **Air bronchograms** = pneumonia; **whiteout** = massive effusion/contusion |
| **ECG** | Ischaemia, arrhythmia, PE signs | Cardiac cause for deterioration | **ST elevation** = STEMI; **S1Q3T3** = PE; **fast AF** = rate-related shock |

**Pattern-recognition finding that confirms escalation need:**  
**Lactate >4 mmol/L + hypotension (SBP <90)** after initial fluid bolus = septic shock → this patient needs ICU-level care, immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics, and likely vasopressors. Do not wait for more tests; escalate now.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – “the golden hour”**

1. **Call for help** – Do not manage alone. Use SBAR:  
   *“I’m Dr X, FY1 on Ward Y. I am calling about Mrs Jones, 72, post-colectomy day 2. She has deteriorated – NEWS2 is 9 (RR 28, SpO₂ 90%, HR 110, BP 90/60, confusion, oliguria). I am worried she is septic and in decompensated shock. Could you please attend urgently? I am starting the sepsis six.”*

2. **Sepsis Six** (if infection suspected):
   - **3 in 1 hour:** Blood cultures, lactate, start IV antibiotics (e.g., **Piperacillin/Tazobactam 4.5g IV** over 30 min, or **Ceftriaxone 2g IV** + **Metronidazole 500mg IV** if penicillin allergy)
   - **3 to complete within 1 hour:** IV fluids (500–1000 mL crystalloid bolus over 15–30 min), urine output monitoring, oxygen to target SpO₂ ≥94%

3. **Fluid resuscitation** – Give **500 mL Hartmann’s or 0.9% NaCl** stat, re-assess. If no improvement (persistent hypotension, rising lactate), this is fluid-resistant shock – needs vasopressors.

4. **Airway and breathing** – If GCS <15 or SpO₂ <90% despite O₂, consider airway support (Guedel, then intubation).

**Ward (24–72h) – after initial stabilisation**

1. **Targeted therapy** – Once cultures available, narrow antibiotics. Monitor lactate clearance: **repeat lactate at 2–4h** – should drop by >20% per hour.
2. **Vasoactive support** – If MAP <65 despite adequate fluid, start **Noradrenaline IV infusion** (titrate to MAP >65). This usually requires ICU – escalate to outreach team.
3. **Source control** – Drain abscess, remove infected lines, re-operate if ischaemic bowel.
4. **Organ support** – Renal replacement therapy (if AKI stage 3), mechanical ventilation (if ARDS). All need ICU.
5. **Nutrition** – Start early enteral feeding once shocked reversed.

**Long-term – during recovery**

1. **Debrief** – Discuss the escalation event with the team – what could have been done sooner? This reduces future error.
2. **Rehabilitation** – Prevent ICU-acquired weakness – physiotherapy, early mobilisation.
3. **Antibiotic duration** – Typically 5–7 days for sepsis; longer for endocarditis, abscess.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**
- Do **not** give a fluid bolus to a patient in pulmonary oedema (listen for crackles; if present, give diuretic instead).
- Do **not** wait for the registrar to call back – keep phoning if no response.
- Do **not** escalate without data – always document NEWS2, bloods, and what you have done so far.
- Do **not** give IV fluids blindly in oliguria without checking for fluid overload (JVP, lung auscultation, CVP if available).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Septic Shock (most common) | Haemorrhagic Shock | Cardiogenic Shock | Anaphylactic Shock |
|---------|----------------------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Pain** | Variable, depends on source | Abdominal pain (if GI bleed), none if occult | Chest pain (MI), epigastric if acute | Itching, throat tightness |
| **Exam** | Warm peripheries initially (vasodilation), then cold; fever/hypothermia | Cold, clammy, pale; may have visible bleeding or distended abdomen | Cold peripheries, crackles, raised JVP, S3 gallop | Urticaria, wheeze, stridor, swelling |
| **ECG** | Non-specific, sinus tachy | Sinus tachy, may have ST depression if anaemic | ST elevation (MI), arrhythmia | Sinus tachy |
| **Labs** | Lactate ↑, WCC ↑/↓, procalcitonin ↑, CRP ↑ | Hb ↓, Hct ↓, normal lactate early then ↑ | Troponin ↑, BNP ↑, lactate ↑ if shock | Tryptase ↑ (if mast cell) |
| **Key diff** | Fever + raised procalcitonin + source of infection | Rapid Hb drop + history of bleeding (surgery, trauma, GI) | Chest pain + ECG changes + elevated troponin + pulmonary oedema | Acute onset with allergen exposure + skin/respiratory signs + hypotension |

*In clinical practice, septic shock is the most common cause of ward deterioration, but never assume – always check for overt bleeding (abdominal distension, drain output) and listen to the chest (oedema vs. consolidation).*
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Complication 1: In-hospital cardiac arrest**  
- **How it presents:** Suddenly – patient becomes pulseless, usually in ventricular fibrillation or asystole. Preceded by rapidly rising NEWS2 (especially RR >30, HR >130, SBP <80).  
- **Key signs (pre-arrest):** Severe tachypnoea, unrecordable BP, GCS <8, agonal breathing.  
- **What to do:** Call cardiac arrest team (2222). Start CPR per ALS protocol. Defibrillate if shockable.  
- **Late-presentation pattern:** Patient “looked fine” 30 minutes ago, then found collapsed – this is classic for missed rapid deterioration (e.g., massive PE, unsuspected haemorrhage).

**Complication 2: Multi-organ dysfunction syndrome (MODS)**  
- **How it presents:** Ongoing shock despite resuscitation. Rising creatinine, bilirubin, INR, platelet drop.  
- **Key signs:** Anuria, jaundice, purpura, prolonged prothrombin time.  
- **What to do:** ICU admission, continuous renal replacement therapy, mechanical ventilation, meticulous fluid management.  
- **Late-presentation pattern:** Sepsis treated on ward with “a bit of IV fluids” – patient stabilises but then develops acute kidney injury and oliguria only recognised later on morning bloods. By then, dialysis may be needed.

**Complication 3: Lactic acidosis-induced cardiac depression**  
- **How it presents:** Refractory hypotension, bradyarrhythmias, progress to asystole.  
- **Key signs:** Rising lactate >8, low pH <7.2, falling MAP.  
- **What to do:** Aggressive fluid, vasopressors, consider sodium bicarbonate (controversial; only if pH <7.15 and no underlying cause resolved).  
- **Late-presentation pattern:** Patient on high-dose noradrenaline, MAP 55, lactate 10. This is end-stage – mortality >80%.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **SBAR** – Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation (use this for every escalation call)  
  - S: “I’m calling about Mrs Jones, NEWS 9.”  
  - B: “Day 2 post-colectomy, known hypertension.”  
  - A: “Lactate 4.5, RR 28, BP 90/60, confused.”  
  - R: “I think she needs ICU outreach; can you come now?”

- **SEPSIS SIX** – 3 in 1 hour (cultures, lactate, antibiotics) + 3 complete in 1 hour (fluids, oxygen, urine output monitor)

- **DETERIORATE** – mnemonic for escalation triggers:  
  - **D** – Drowsy/confused (GCS drop)  
  - **E** – Elevated respiratory rate (>24)  
  - **T** – Tachy (>110) or brady (<50)  
  - **E** – Excessive breathing (SpO₂ <90% on O₂)  
  - **R** – Renal (urine <0.5 mL/kg/h for 2h)  
  - **I** – Increased lactate (>2)  
  - **O** – Oxygen need rising  
  - **R** – Recurrent NEWS ≥5  
  - **A** – Any abnormal ABG  
  - **T** – Temperature <36 or >38.5  
  - **E** – ECG new arrhythmia/ischaemia

**High-yield pearls:**
1. **Tachypnoea is the first sign of deterioration** – always count RR yourself; nurses do it accurately.
2. **Lactate is your best friend** – a single lactate >4 in a patient without known liver disease is a hard trigger to escalate to outreach.
3. **A NEWS2 ≥7 is a medical emergency** – treat it like a cardiac arrest in terms of urgency.
4. **“Call for help early, even if you’re not sure”** – because you’re not expected to manage a decompensating patient alone; seniors appreciate early heads-up.
5. **If the patient looks unwell, escalate** – even if vitals are borderline. Your clinical gut is a valid signal.
6. **Hypotension is a late sign** – do not wait for it. Escalate on rising lactate, falling urine output, or confused patient before the BP drops.
7. **Never give IV fluids to a patient with pulmonary oedema** – listen with a stethoscope. Crackles = stop fluids, consider diuretic.

**Common ward mistakes to avoid:**
- **“They just need more time”** – If the patient has not improved after one hour of treatment (fluids, antibiotics) and lactate is not falling, that is failure. Escalate.
- **“The registrar is busy”** – Kepp phoning every 10 minutes if the patient is deteriorating. It is your duty to escalate until help arrives.
- **“I’ll fix it with just a fluid bolus”** – If the BP does not improve after 1L, do not give a second litre without senior review. The problem may be vasodilatory (needs vasopressor) or cardiogenic (needs diuretic).
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call a senior (registrar or consultant) / ICU outreach / crash team:**

| Scenario | Threshold | Who to call |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **NEWS2 score** | ≥5 (any single parameter = 3 or total ≥5) | Ward registrar immediately; if no response within 15 min, call outreach |
| **NEWS2 score** | ≥7 (regardless of single parameters) | Critical care outreach / ICU registrar NOW |
| **Lactate** | >4 mmol/L (without resolved trigger) | ICU outreach |
| **GCS** | Drop by 2 points or <9 | Medical emergency team / anaesthetics |
| **Respiratory** | RR >30 or <8, SpO₂ <90% despite 100% O₂, requiring NIV | Outreach / ICU |
| **Cardiovascular** | SBP <90 despite fluid bolus, HR >130 or <40, new arrhythmia with haemodynamic instability | ICU / cardiology registrar |
| **Renal** | Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 hours after fluid challenge | Renal / ICU |
| **Any single red flag** (e.g., stridor, anaphylaxis, status epilepticus, massive haemorrhage) | Immediately | Crash team (2222) |

**Specific thresholds (pre-arrest):**
- **RR <8 or >30** + **HR <40 or >140** + **SBP <80** = imminent cardiac arrest – call 2222.
- **GCS <9** with protected airway failure – call anaesthetics.

**Remember:** You are never punished for escalating too early. You *are* punished for delaying. When in doubt, call. That is the safest decision you can make for your patient.

## Escalation of Care — Decision

**One-liner:** A structured process to identify and respond to clinical deterioration, activating senior or critical care resources to prevent cardiac arrest or avoidable harm.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**
A 72-year-old woman, day 2 post-hemicolectomy for colon cancer, is reviewed by the night FY1. She is mildly confused, her respiratory rate is 28, oxygen saturations 90% on 4L nasal cannula, heart rate 110, blood pressure 90/60, temperature 38.5°C, and urine output <20 mL/hour for the last 2 hours. The nursing staff are worried. The FY1 feels out of depth and considers whether to call the registrar.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Confusion** → cerebral hypoperfusion / hypoxia / sepsis-associated encephalopathy – a red flag for organ dysfunction
- **Respiratory rate 28** → earliest and most sensitive sign of deterioration (tachypnoea compensates for metabolic acidosis)
- **SpO₂ 90% on 4L** → gas exchange failure; likely intrapulmonary shunting or worsening pneumonia/ARDS
- **HR 110, BP 90/60** → compensated shock; sympathetic drive is maximal but losing ground
- **Temperature 38.5°C** → infective trigger (though hypothermia also = bad)
- **Oliguria (<20 mL/hr)** → renal hypoperfusion (pre-renal AKI); near-failing perfusion
- **Nursing staff worried** → never ignore clinical instinct from the bedside team – often the earliest pattern cue

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Silent hypoxia:** young patient with COVID-19, saturations 85% but completely alert, tachypnoeic but not distressed. Escalation delayed because patient “looks well” – but RR >24 and SpO₂ <90% is a hard trigger.
- **Isolated hypotension post-op:** BP 75/40, alert, no tachycardia. Could be vasoplegia from spinal anaesthetic or bleeding into drains. Both require escalation – if you assume “it’s just the spinal” you miss haemorrhage.
- **Agitation/psychosis without vital sign change:** elderly patient on opioids becomes restless. Maybe hypoxia or hypercapnia – check ABG before sedating. Early escalation to acute physicians can prevent aspiration.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Escalation of Care is the explicit decision to hand over or share decision-making about a patient whose clinical trajectory is worsening beyond the ability (or scope) of the current team. It is a multi-step judgment that includes recognising deterioration, communicating concern, and activating a higher level of care – typically a senior ward clinician, an outreach team, or intensive care.

**Why it matters:**  
- **Frequency:** ~1 in 20 acute admissions will deteriorate unexpectedly; failure to escalate is the third commonest cause of in-hospital cardiac arrest.
- **Stakes:** Missed escalation leads to preventable ICU admission delay, prolonged organ failure, or death. Conversely, inappropriate “fake escalations” (e.g., calling ICU for a mild UTI) waste resources and erode trust.
- **Common pitfalls:** Overconfidence (“I can fix it myself”), hierarchical fear (“the registrar will be annoyed I called”), and pattern-blindness (“they looked fine an hour ago”). These are **decision failures**, not knowledge gaps – so you need a structured mental model.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

The decision to escalate is driven by **pathophysiological decompensation**, usually from an inflammatory or hypoperfusion state that overwhelms compensatory mechanisms.

**The story:**
- A trigger (infection, bleeding, hypovolaemia, MI) activates the sympathetic nervous system and innate immune response. Catecholamines increase heart rate, contractility, and vasoconstriction. This is **compensated shock** – the patient may be tachycardic, hypertensive, and tachypnoeic. At this stage, early intervention (fluids, antibiotics) can reverse.
- If the insult persists, oxygen delivery falls below demand. Anaerobic metabolism generates lactate, causing metabolic acidosis. The respiratory centre drives tachypnoea (Kussmaul breathing) to blow off CO₂ – this is why **tachypnoea is the earliest sign**.
- As acidosis worsens, myocardial depression sets in. Vasodilation (from cytokines or sepsis) overcomes compensatory vasoconstriction. Blood pressure drops – **hypotension is a late sign** (“the patient has exhausted their reserves”).
- Organs suffer: brain (confusion), kidneys (oliguria), liver (transaminitis, coagulopathy), lungs (ARDS). **The presence of any single organ dysfunction = the patient is no longer compensating**.
- Eventually, ATP depletion causes cell death and irreversible shock. This is why **time matters** – your window to escalate is narrow.

**Mechanism → clinical signs:**
- Reduced cerebral perfusion → confusion, agitation, reduced GCS
- Increased lactate → metabolic acidosis → compensatory tachypnoea
- Renal hypoperfusion → prerenal AKI → oliguria, rising creatinine
- Inadequate oxygen delivery → cyanosis, mottled skin (late)
- Anaerobic metabolism → lactic acidosis → high anion gap on ABG

**Connect to pattern cues:**
In the vignette, the patient has three organ dysfunctions (CNS, renal, respiratory). That is a **red-flag pattern** – she has already decompensated. The FY1 cannot manage this alone; escalation is mandatory.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Infection** | Sepsis (pneumonia, UTI, intra-abdominal, line-related) | Fever/hypothermia + raised WCC/procalcitonin + focal signs |
| **Hypovolaemia** | Haemorrhage (post-op, GI bleed, trauma), dehydration (vomiting, diarrhoea, burns) | Tachycardia, low CVP, drop in Hb, obvious loss |
| **Cardiovascular** | Myocardial infarction, arrhythmia (AF, VT), tamponade, pulmonary embolism | Chest pain, ECG changes, raised troponin, D-dimer |
| **Respiratory** | Pneumothorax, pulmonary oedema, COPD exacerbation, massive PE | Unilateral breath sounds, frothy sputum, known COPD |
| **Metabolic/endocrine** | Diabetic ketoacidosis, Addisonian crisis, hypoglycaemia, thyroid storm | History of diabetes/steroid use, hyperventilation, electrolyte disturbances |
| **Neurological** | Stroke, seizure (status epilepticus), raised ICP, meningitis | Focal neurology, seizure activity, reduced GCS, fever |
| **Toxicological** | Drug overdose (opioids, benzos, digoxin), anaphylaxis, transfusion reaction | Known substance use, laryngeal oedema, skin rash, hypotension |
| **Procedural** | Failed intubation, massive haemorrhage during surgery, post-procedural bleeding | Recent procedure, low BP, high lactate |

**Most common cause** in a general ward: **Sepsis** – triggers a systemic inflammatory response that can cause multi-organ failure within hours.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
**NEWS2 (National Early Warning Score 2)** – the most widely used escalation tool.  
- Score 0–2: normal → routine monitoring  
- Score 3–4: trigger for urgent team review

**For each key test:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|---------------|------------------|

| **Blood cultures** | Bacteraemia | Identify pathogen before antibiotics | **Two sets from different sites** – positive = sepsis source; but do not delay ABx |
| **FBC** | WCC (↑/↓), Hb (↓ in bleeding), platelets | Infection response, anaemia, DIC | **WCC <4 or >20** = high risk sepsis. **Platelets <100** = possible DIC |
| **U&Es** | Creatinine, urea, potassium | Renal function, hydration | **Creatinine ↑ by >26 µmol/L in 48h = AKI Stage 1** → needs fluid and stop nephrotoxins |
| **Lactate** | Anaerobic metabolism | Single best predictor of deterioration | **Lactate >2** is abnormal; >4 = severe sepsis/septic shock → escalate |

| **ECG** | Ischaemia, arrhythmia, PE signs | Cardiac cause for deterioration | **ST elevation** = STEMI; **S1Q3T3** = PE; **fast AF** = rate-related shock |

**Pattern-recognition finding that confirms escalation need:**

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – “the golden hour”**

1. **Call for help** – Do not manage alone. Use SBAR:  
   *“I’m Dr X, FY1 on Ward Y. I am calling about Mrs Jones, 72, post-colectomy day 2. She has deteriorated – NEWS2 is 9 (RR 28, SpO₂ 90%, HR 110, BP 90/60, confusion, oliguria). I am worried she is septic and in decompensated shock. Could you please attend urgently? I am starting the sepsis six.”*

2. **Sepsis Six** (if infection suspected):
   - **3 in 1 hour:** Blood cultures, lactate, start IV antibiotics (e.g., **Piperacillin/Tazobactam 4.5g IV** over 30 min, or **Ceftriaxone 2g IV** + **Metronidazole 500mg IV** if penicillin allergy)
   - **3 to complete within 1 hour:** IV fluids (500–1000 mL crystalloid bolus over 15–30 min), urine output monitoring, oxygen to target SpO₂ ≥94%

3. **Fluid resuscitation** – Give **500 mL Hartmann’s or 0.9% NaCl** stat, re-assess. If no improvement (persistent hypotension, rising lactate), this is fluid-resistant shock – needs vasopressors.

4. **Airway and breathing** – If GCS <15 or SpO₂ <90% despite O₂, consider airway support (Guedel, then intubation).

**Ward (24–72h) – after initial stabilisation**

1. **Targeted therapy** – Once cultures available, narrow antibiotics. Monitor lactate clearance: **repeat lactate at 2–4h** – should drop by >20% per hour.
2. **Vasoactive support** – If MAP <65 despite adequate fluid, start **Noradrenaline IV infusion** (titrate to MAP >65). This usually requires ICU – escalate to outreach team.
3. **Source control** – Drain abscess, remove infected lines, re-operate if ischaemic bowel.
4. **Organ support** – Renal replacement therapy (if AKI stage 3), mechanical ventilation (if ARDS). All need ICU.
5. **Nutrition** – Start early enteral feeding once shocked reversed.

**Long-term – during recovery**

1. **Debrief** – Discuss the escalation event with the team – what could have been done sooner? This reduces future error.
2. **Rehabilitation** – Prevent ICU-acquired weakness – physiotherapy, early mobilisation.
3. **Antibiotic duration** – Typically 5–7 days for sepsis; longer for endocarditis, abscess.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**
- Do **not** give a fluid bolus to a patient in pulmonary oedema (listen for crackles; if present, give diuretic instead).
- Do **not** wait for the registrar to call back – keep phoning if no response.
- Do **not** escalate without data – always document NEWS2, bloods, and what you have done so far.
- Do **not** give IV fluids blindly in oliguria without checking for fluid overload (JVP, lung auscultation, CVP if available).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Septic Shock (most common) | Haemorrhagic Shock | Cardiogenic Shock | Anaphylactic Shock |
|---------|----------------------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Pain** | Variable, depends on source | Abdominal pain (if GI bleed), none if occult | Chest pain (MI), epigastric if acute | Itching, throat tightness |
| **Exam** | Warm peripheries initially (vasodilation), then cold; fever/hypothermia | Cold, clammy, pale; may have visible bleeding or distended abdomen | Cold peripheries, crackles, raised JVP, S3 gallop | Urticaria, wheeze, stridor, swelling |
| **ECG** | Non-specific, sinus tachy | Sinus tachy, may have ST depression if anaemic | ST elevation (MI), arrhythmia | Sinus tachy |
| **Labs** | Lactate ↑, WCC ↑/↓, procalcitonin ↑, CRP ↑ | Hb ↓, Hct ↓, normal lactate early then ↑ | Troponin ↑, BNP ↑, lactate ↑ if shock | Tryptase ↑ (if mast cell) |
| **Key diff** | Fever + raised procalcitonin + source of infection | Rapid Hb drop + history of bleeding (surgery, trauma, GI) | Chest pain + ECG changes + elevated troponin + pulmonary oedema | Acute onset with allergen exposure + skin/respiratory signs + hypotension |

*In clinical practice, septic shock is the most common cause of ward deterioration, but never assume – always check for overt bleeding (abdominal distension, drain output) and listen to the chest (oedema vs. consolidation).*

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Complication 1: In-hospital cardiac arrest**  
- **How it presents:** Suddenly – patient becomes pulseless, usually in ventricular fibrillation or asystole. Preceded by rapidly rising NEWS2 (especially RR >30, HR >130, SBP <80).  
- **Key signs (pre-arrest):** Severe tachypnoea, unrecordable BP, GCS <8, agonal breathing.  
- **What to do:** Call cardiac arrest team (2222). Start CPR per ALS protocol. Defibrillate if shockable.  
- **Late-presentation pattern:** Patient “looked fine” 30 minutes ago, then found collapsed – this is classic for missed rapid deterioration (e.g., massive PE, unsuspected haemorrhage).

**Complication 2: Multi-organ dysfunction syndrome (MODS)**  
- **How it presents:** Ongoing shock despite resuscitation. Rising creatinine, bilirubin, INR, platelet drop.  
- **Key signs:** Anuria, jaundice, purpura, prolonged prothrombin time.  
- **What to do:** ICU admission, continuous renal replacement therapy, mechanical ventilation, meticulous fluid management.  
- **Late-presentation pattern:** Sepsis treated on ward with “a bit of IV fluids” – patient stabilises but then develops acute kidney injury and oliguria only recognised later on morning bloods. By then, dialysis may be needed.

**Complication 3: Lactic acidosis-induced cardiac depression**  
- **How it presents:** Refractory hypotension, bradyarrhythmias, progress to asystole.  
- **Key signs:** Rising lactate >8, low pH <7.2, falling MAP.  
- **What to do:** Aggressive fluid, vasopressors, consider sodium bicarbonate (controversial; only if pH <7.15 and no underlying cause resolved).  
- **Late-presentation pattern:** Patient on high-dose noradrenaline, MAP 55, lactate 10. This is end-stage – mortality >80%.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **SBAR** – Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation (use this for every escalation call)  
  - S: “I’m calling about Mrs Jones, NEWS 9.”  
  - B: “Day 2 post-colectomy, known hypertension.”  
  - A: “Lactate 4.5, RR 28, BP 90/60, confused.”  
  - R: “I think she needs ICU outreach; can you come now?”

- **SEPSIS SIX** – 3 in 1 hour (cultures, lactate, antibiotics) + 3 complete in 1 hour (fluids, oxygen, urine output monitor)

- **DETERIORATE** – mnemonic for escalation triggers:  
  - **D** – Drowsy/confused (GCS drop)  
  - **E** – Elevated respiratory rate (>24)  
  - **T** – Tachy (>110) or brady (<50)  
  - **E** – Excessive breathing (SpO₂ <90% on O₂)  
  - **R** – Renal (urine <0.5 mL/kg/h for 2h)  
  - **I** – Increased lactate (>2)  
  - **O** – Oxygen need rising  
  - **R** – Recurrent NEWS ≥5  
  - **A** – Any abnormal ABG  
  - **T** – Temperature <36 or >38.5  
  - **E** – ECG new arrhythmia/ischaemia

**High-yield pearls:**
1. **Tachypnoea is the first sign of deterioration** – always count RR yourself; nurses do it accurately.
2. **Lactate is your best friend** – a single lactate >4 in a patient without known liver disease is a hard trigger to escalate to outreach.
3. **A NEWS2 ≥7 is a medical emergency** – treat it like a cardiac arrest in terms of urgency.
4. **“Call for help early, even if you’re not sure”** – because you’re not expected to manage a decompensating patient alone; seniors appreciate early heads-up.
5. **If the patient looks unwell, escalate** – even if vitals are borderline. Your clinical gut is a valid signal.
6. **Hypotension is a late sign** – do not wait for it. Escalate on rising lactate, falling urine output, or confused patient before the BP drops.
7. **Never give IV fluids to a patient with pulmonary oedema** – listen with a stethoscope. Crackles = stop fluids, consider diuretic.

**Common ward mistakes to avoid:**
- **“They just need more time”** – If the patient has not improved after one hour of treatment (fluids, antibiotics) and lactate is not falling, that is failure. Escalate.
- **“The registrar is busy”** – Kepp phoning every 10 minutes if the patient is deteriorating. It is your duty to escalate until help arrives.
- **“I’ll fix it with just a fluid bolus”** – If the BP does not improve after 1L, do not give a second litre without senior review. The problem may be vasodilatory (needs vasopressor) or cardiogenic (needs diuretic).

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call a senior (registrar or consultant) / ICU outreach / crash team:**

| Scenario | Threshold | Who to call |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **NEWS2 score** | ≥5 (any single parameter = 3 or total ≥5) | Ward registrar immediately; if no response within 15 min, call outreach |
| **NEWS2 score** | ≥7 (regardless of single parameters) | Critical care outreach / ICU registrar NOW |
| **Lactate** | >4 mmol/L (without resolved trigger) | ICU outreach |
| **GCS** | Drop by 2 points or <9 | Medical emergency team / anaesthetics |
| **Respiratory** | RR >30 or <8, SpO₂ <90% despite 100% O₂, requiring NIV | Outreach / ICU |
| **Cardiovascular** | SBP <90 despite fluid bolus, HR >130 or <40, new arrhythmia with haemodynamic instability | ICU / cardiology registrar |
| **Renal** | Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 hours after fluid challenge | Renal / ICU |
| **Any single red flag** (e.g., stridor, anaphylaxis, status epilepticus, massive haemorrhage) | Immediately | Crash team (2222) |

**Specific thresholds (pre-arrest):**
- **RR <8 or >30** + **HR <40 or >140** + **SBP <80** = imminent cardiac arrest – call 2222.
- **GCS <9** with protected airway failure – call anaesthetics.

**Remember:** You are never punished for escalating too early. You *are* punished for delaying. When in doubt, call. That is the safest decision you can make for your patient.
